title: Dialing in a Stable Current
learningObjectives: Tune voltage and resistance together to hit a target current
Use higher resistance to stabilize current when voltage changes
Observe how small voltage tweaks shift series current
introText: Precision circuits need predictable current. You'll pair a moderate source with higher resistance, then tweak both until the current holds steady in a safe band.

steps.instructionText: Place a DC source and a resistor in series, then wire them into a closed loop.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Loop is closed and ready for measurement.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Wire the source and resistor end-to-end so every terminal is connected.

steps.instructionText: Set the source to about 10V and the resistor near 820Ω so current stays below 15 mA.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current is under 15 mA — safely within many sensor limits.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Raise the resistor or lower the voltage until current drops below 15 mA.

steps.instructionText: Nudge the source voltage slightly while keeping current under 15 mA to confirm stability.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Great — small voltage changes didn't push current over the limit.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: If current rises past 15 mA, increase resistance or drop the voltage.

questions.questionText: If voltage rises slightly in a series circuit, what happens to current?
questions.explanation: With fixed resistance, higher voltage means higher current (I = V/R).

questions.questionText: Why use higher resistance for a stable current budget?
options: Higher resistance reduces current changes from small voltage shifts
questions.explanation: A larger resistor lowers current and makes small voltage changes less likely to exceed limits.
